generatedNote: Recomputable from results/raw/. Candidate verdicts vs ground truth -> metrics.mjs.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Perils Insured Against, Peril 1: Fire or smoke
matrix.rationale: The proximate cause is fire, a named covered peril. No exclusions apply: the dwelling was occupied, no flood or earth movement contributed, and no wear-and-tear or seepage chain is present. The standard $1,000 deductible applies (windstorm deductible is not triggered); no sub-limits are implicated because the loss is structural/cleaning rather than jewelry, electronics, or other sub-limited categories. $40,000 minus $1,000 deductible yields $39,000 payable.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions (a): Water damage — flood, surface water, overflow of a body of water
matrix.rationale: The loss is caused by a river cresting and floodwater inundating the dwelling — textbook flood and overflow of a body of water, explicitly excluded under (a). The anti-concurrent-causation provision reinforces this: the exclusion applies regardless of any other contributing cause or sequence. No named insured peril is the proximate cause, the ensuing-loss carve-out does not apply to flood (it is limited to wear-and-tear and mold), and there is no coverage to extend.

matrix.controlling: Exclusion (a) Water damage
matrix.rationale: The loss was caused by the cresting of a river and a regional flood event, which is explicitly excluded under the water damage exclusion for flood, surface water, and overflow of a body of water.

matrix.controlling: Perils Insured Against 1. Fire or smoke
matrix.rationale: The damage was directly caused by a kitchen fire and resulting smoke, which are named covered perils. The property was occupied and no exclusions apply, so the full $40,000 estimate is covered minus the standard $1,000 deductible.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Perils Insured Against: Theft
matrix.rationale: The loss of the road bike by theft is a covered peril, and bicycles are not subject to any special sub-limits. The claim is covered in full minus the $1,000 standard deductible.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (e) Intentional loss>
matrix.rationale: The claim involves an intentional act of theft, which is explicitly excluded under Section I — Exclusions (e). The ensuing-loss provision does not apply since the original cause of loss was intentional. Therefore, we refer this claim for investigation to determine if any other coverage applies.

matrix.controlling: Section I Peril 3 (theft); $1,000 standard deductible
matrix.rationale: The cut lock and police report establish theft under covered Peril 3. A road bike is general personal property — none of the enumerated sub-limits (jewelry, firearms, electronics, business property) apply. Covered loss of $3,000 less the $1,000 standard deductible yields $2,000 payable to the insured.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Perils Insured Against: Fire or smoke
matrix.rationale: The loss is direct physical damage from a kitchen stovetop fire and resultant smoke, both named covered perils. No exclusion applies (dwelling was occupied, no indication of intentional act, wear/tear, or other excluded cause), and the repair estimate is not subject to any special sub-limit. The standard $1,000 deductible applies after the full covered loss.

matrix.controlling: Section I Perils Insured Against: fire or smoke; standard $1,000 deductible
matrix.rationale: The direct physical loss was caused by a stovetop fire and resulting smoke, both named covered perils. No exclusion or sub-limit applies, so the $40,000 covered loss is paid less the $1,000 standard deductible.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusions (a) Water damage: flood/overflow of a body of water excluded regardless of concurrent causes
matrix.rationale: The loss was caused by a regional river flood/overflow of a body of water, which is expressly excluded. The anti-concurrent-causation provision bars coverage, and no ensuing-loss carve-out applies to flood.

matrix.controlling: Perils Insured Against #3: Theft
matrix.rationale: The cut shed lock and police report support theft, a named covered peril. No bicycle sub-limit applies, so the $3,000 covered loss is reduced only by the $1,000 standard deductible.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusions (a) — Water damage (flood/surface water/overflow of a body of water)
matrix.rationale: The loss arose from a regional flood when the river crested and inundated the ground floor; that is excluded flood and overflow of a body of water. The anti-concurrent-causation provision bars coverage regardless of any other contributing cause, and the ensuing-loss carve-out applies only to wear and tear and mold, not flood.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Perils Insured Against (3) Theft
matrix.rationale: The road bike was stolen from the insured's shed after the lock was cut, establishing theft as the proximate cause—a named covered peril. The bike is not subject to any listed sub-limit, so the $3,000 loss is reduced only by the $1,000 standard deductible.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (c) Wear and tear>
matrix.rationale: The claim involves water damage from a regional flood event, which is excluded under Section I — Exclusions (a) Water damage. The ensuing-loss provision does not apply since the primary cause of loss is an excluded peril. Therefore, we refer for investigation to determine the proximate cause of the loss.

matrix.controlling: Special limits of liability — $1,500 on jewelry, watches, and furs for loss by theft only; Standard deductible $1,000
matrix.rationale: The break-in is a covered theft peril. However, the jewelry/watches sub-limit caps recovery at $1,500 before the deductible is applied. After subtracting the $1,000 standard deductible from the $1,500 sub-limit, the net payable is $500. The remaining $3,500 of appraised value above the sub-limit is not recoverable under this policy.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions (b): Earth movement, anti-concurrent-causation provision
matrix.rationale: The proximate cause of both the foundation crack and the chimney collapse is an earthquake, which is explicitly excluded under Exclusion (b) as 'earth movement.' The anti-concurrent-causation provision bars coverage regardless of any other contributing cause or sequence of events. The ensuing-loss carve-out does not apply — it is limited to exclusions (c) wear and tear and (d) mold only, and does not restore coverage for loss ensuing from earth movement.

matrix.controlling: Special limits of liability: $2,500 on electronics, applied before the $1,000 standard deductible
matrix.rationale: Fire is a covered peril under Section I. The electronics sub-limit caps recovery at $2,500 regardless of the $4,000 actual loss. Applying the $1,000 standard deductible after the sub-limit yields $1,500 net to the insured. The theft-only jewelry/firearms sub-limits do not apply; the electronics sub-limit applies to all covered perils.

matrix.controlling: SECTION I — EXCLUSIONS (b)
matrix.rationale: The loss was caused by an earthquake, which is explicitly excluded under the earth movement provision, and the ensuing-loss exception does not apply to earth movement.

matrix.controlling: Special limits of liability (sub-limits)
matrix.rationale: Fire is a covered peril, but the loss is capped by the $2,500 electronics sub-limit. After applying the $1,000 standard deductible to the sub-limit, the payable amount is $1,500.

matrix.controlling: Special limits of liability (sub-limits)
matrix.rationale: Theft of jewelry and watches is a covered peril but subject to a $1,500 sub-limit. Applying the standard $1,000 deductible after the sub-limit leaves a net payable amount of $500.

matrix.controlling: Special limits: $1,500 on jewelry, watches, and furs for loss by theft only; deductible applied after sub-limit
matrix.rationale: The burglary is a covered theft loss, and the claimed rings and watch fall under the jewelry/watches theft sub-limit. The $1,500 sub-limit applies before the $1,000 standard deductible, leaving $500 payable.

matrix.controlling: Special limits of liability: $2,500 on electronics, applied before deductible
matrix.rationale: Fire is a named covered peril, but the TV, receiver, and consoles are electronics subject to the $2,500 electronics sub-limit. The $1,000 standard deductible applies after that sub-limit, leaving $1,500 payable.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusions (b) — Earth movement (anti-concurrent causation)
matrix.rationale: The insured identifies earthquake as the cause: foundation cracking and chimney collapse through the roof are direct physical loss from earth movement, an excluded peril. Anti-concurrent causation bars coverage regardless of any secondary mechanism (e.g., falling chimney), and the ensuing-loss carve-out applies only to wear/tear and mold, not earth movement.

matrix.controlling: Special limits: $1,500 on jewelry, watches, and furs — for loss by theft only
matrix.rationale: Burglary of the jewelry box is a covered theft peril, supported by the police report. Rings and a watch are subject to the $1,500 theft sub-limit on jewelry and watches, not the full $5,000 appraisal. After applying that sub-limit, the $1,000 standard deductible leaves $500 net payable.

matrix.controlling: Special limits of liability — $2,500 on electronics
matrix.rationale: Fire is a named covered peril and is the clear proximate cause of the home theater electronics loss. The $2,500 electronics sub-limit caps the covered amount before the standard $1,000 deductible, so $4,000 in claimed electronics is limited to $2,500 and then reduced to $1,500 net.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusions b, Earth movement with anti-concurrent-causation
matrix.rationale: The described proximate cause is earthquake, which is excluded as earth movement regardless of any contributing or sequential covered peril. The ensuing-loss carve-out does not apply to earth movement.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (c) wear and tear>
matrix.rationale: The loss of the TV, receiver, and consoles is excluded under Exclusion (c) wear and tear because they were damaged by constant or repeated seepage or leakage over a period of fourteen days or more. The ensuing-loss provision does not restore coverage for this type of damage.

matrix.controlling: Standard deductible: $1,000 (applied after any sub-limit)
matrix.rationale: Theft is a covered peril and the bike is personal property with no applicable sub-limit. However, the $800 loss does not exceed the $1,000 standard deductible, so no amount is payable to the insured.

matrix.controlling: Windstorm deductible $2,500 applies as proximate covered peril is windstorm
matrix.rationale: Wind is a covered peril (Section I, Peril 2). The water intrusion is a direct consequence of the windstorm opening the roof — not an independent flood or seepage event — so Exclusion (a) does not apply. The $3,000 roofer quote is fully covered, but the windstorm deductible of $2,500 applies, leaving $500 payable to the insured.

matrix.controlling: SECTION I — CONDITIONS, Deductible
matrix.rationale: The claimed loss of $800 for the stolen bike is covered under the theft peril, but it falls below the standard policy deductible of $1,000, resulting in no payable amount.

matrix.controlling: Windstorm or hail deductible
matrix.rationale: The proximate cause is a windstorm, a covered peril, causing $3,000 in damage. After applying the $2,500 windstorm deductible, the net payable amount is $500.

matrix.controlling: <SECTION I — PERILS INSURED AGAINST (3) Theft>
matrix.rationale: The theft of the bike is covered under Section I — Perils Insured Against (3) Theft. The deductible applies, but since the loss exceeds $1,000, we pay the full amount minus the deductible.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions (Anti-concurrent causation / Water damage)
matrix.rationale: The damage was caused by a combination of windstorm (covered) and storm surge (excluded). Under the anti-concurrent causation provision, because the excluded peril of water damage contributed concurrently or in sequence, the entire loss is excluded.

matrix.controlling: Section I Conditions - $1,000 standard deductible
matrix.rationale: Theft is a covered peril and no listed sub-limit applies to the bicycle. However, the $800 covered loss does not exceed the $1,000 standard deductible, so no payment is due.

matrix.controlling: Perils Insured Against: Windstorm or hail; windstorm deductible $2,500
matrix.rationale: Straight-line wind is a named covered peril and caused the roof damage and resulting attic water intrusion. The $3,000 covered loss is reduced by the applicable $2,500 windstorm deductible, leaving $500 payable.

matrix.controlling: Standard deductible ($1,000)
matrix.rationale: Theft of the bicycle from the porch is a covered peril, and a bike is not subject to any special sub-limit. The claimed loss of $800 does not exceed the $1,000 standard deductible, so no amount is payable.

matrix.controlling: Windstorm or hail deductible ($2,500)
matrix.rationale: Proximate cause is windstorm: straight-line winds removed shingles and rain entered the attic through that breach before tarping. Windstorm is a covered peril; exclusion (a) addresses flood/surface water/storm surge, not rain intrusion through wind-caused roof damage. No sub-limits apply; $3,000 loss minus the $2,500 windstorm deductible yields $500 net.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusions (a) Water damage with anti-concurrent-causation provision
matrix.rationale: Storm surge is excluded water damage, and the anti-concurrent-causation provision excludes loss caused directly or indirectly by storm surge regardless of wind contributing concurrently or in sequence. The submitted estimate is not separated into a distinct covered wind portion, so no payable covered amount can be determined from the claim as presented.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Conditions: Determination of cause; and Exclusion (a) storm surge
matrix.rationale: Windstorm is a named covered peril; storm surge is expressly excluded under Exclusion (a) ('storm surge … whether or not driven by wind') and the anti-concurrent-causation provision does not restore it. In principle the covered wind loss (siding, roof) and the excluded storm-surge loss (first-floor flooding) are legally distinct — the ACC clause bars recovery of the storm-surge portion, not the wind portion — but the insured has acknowledged the $30,000 estimate cannot be broken down between the two perils. Because the payable (wind) amount is indeterminate from the facts presented, the claim must be referred for an on-site adjuster to segregate covered wind damage from excluded storm-surge damage before any coverage decision or payment can be issued.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions, anti-concurrent-causation provision
matrix.rationale: Windstorm is a named covered peril, but storm surge is excluded water damage under exclusion (a). Both perils damaged the dwelling within the same hour, so the excluded peril contributed concurrently to the loss. Under the anti-concurrent-causation provision, that contribution bars coverage for the entire claim even though wind also occurred; the ensuing-loss carve-out does not apply to flood.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions (d): Mold, fungus, wet or dry rot
matrix.rationale: The loss is directly and solely mold damage, which is explicitly excluded under Section I(d). The ensuing-loss carve-out does not apply here because it only restores coverage for a separate ensuing loss caused by an insured peril — the mold itself is the claimed damage, not a downstream consequence of it. No covered peril is identified as having ensued from the mold.

matrix.controlling: Exclusion (d) Mold
matrix.rationale: The policy explicitly excludes loss caused directly or indirectly by mold, fungus, or rot. The claim is for mold remediation, and there is no separate ensuing loss by a covered peril to restore coverage.

matrix.controlling: Earth movement exclusion with anti-concurrent causation
matrix.rationale: The fire was indirectly caused by an earthquake. The anti-concurrent causation provision excludes any loss caused directly or indirectly by earth movement, and the ensuing-loss clause explicitly does not restore coverage for loss ensuing from earth movement.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions (b) Earth movement + Anti-Concurrent-Causation provision
matrix.rationale: The causal chain is earthquake → gas line rupture → fire; earth movement is an excluded peril, and the ACC provision bars coverage for loss caused by an excluded peril regardless of any other cause contributing in any sequence. The ensuing-loss carve-out cannot rescue this claim because it applies only to losses ensuing from wear and tear (c) or mold (d), and explicitly does not restore coverage for loss that ensues from earth movement.

matrix.controlling: Ensuing loss
matrix.rationale: The pipe's corrosion is excluded under the wear and tear provision, meaning the $1,500 to replace the pipe itself is not covered. However, the sudden burst of water is covered as an ensuing loss, so the $6,000 for water damage is payable minus the $1,000 standard deductible.

matrix.controlling: Ensuing-loss carve-out under Exclusion (c): water damage to floors and cabinets covered as ensuing loss from corrosion; corroded pipe itself excluded.
matrix.rationale: The pipe's failure is rooted in corrosion/deterioration, an excluded cause under Section I Exclusion (c). However, the ensuing-loss provision expressly restores coverage when an excluded (c) cause produces a separate ensuing loss by a covered peril — here, sudden and accidental discharge of water (Peril 4) — so the $6,000 to dry out and replace floors and cabinets is covered. The $1,500 plumber's bill to replace the corroded pipe section is explicitly not covered, as the carve-out excludes 'the cost to repair or replace the worn, defective … property that was the original excluded cause.' Applying the standard $1,000 deductible to the $6,000 covered loss (no applicable sub-limits) yields $5,000 net payable.

matrix.controlling: (c) wear and tear or (g) vacancy exclusions
matrix.rationale: The claim involves a loss caused by an excluded peril (earth movement), which is not covered. However, the proximate cause of the fire cannot be reasonably determined from the facts presented, as it is unclear whether the earthquake was the direct or indirect cause of the fire. Therefore, the claim is referred for investigation to determine the actual cause and make a coverage decision.

matrix.controlling: Earth movement exclusion with anti-concurrent causation; ensuing-loss carve-out does not apply to earth movement
matrix.rationale: The fire damage arose from a gas line rupture caused by an earthquake. Earth movement is excluded regardless of any covered peril contributing in sequence, and the ensuing-loss provision restores coverage only for wear and tear or mold, not earthquake.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusions (d) mold, fungus, wet or dry rot
matrix.rationale: The claimed loss is black mold remediation and mold damage to subfloor/wall, which is expressly excluded. The ensuing-loss carve-out does not apply because no separate ensuing loss by a covered peril is described.

matrix.controlling: Ensuing loss: wear/corrosion excluded, but separate ensuing loss by insured peril is covered; defective pipe not covered
matrix.rationale: The corroded pipe itself is excluded as wear, tear, deterioration, or corrosion. The sudden burst caused separate ensuing water damage to floors and cabinets, a covered peril, so $6,000 is covered and the $1,000 standard deductible applies, net $5,000.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusion (d) — Mold, fungus, wet or dry rot
matrix.rationale: The claimed $8,000 loss is mold remediation for black mold in the subfloor and wall, which is loss caused directly by mold and is expressly excluded. The anti-concurrent-causation provision bars coverage even if a covered peril such as sudden plumbing discharge contributed, and the ensuing-loss carve-out applies only to separate ensuing damage by a covered peril—not the cost to remediate the moldy property itself.

matrix.controlling: Ensuing loss carve-out (Section I Exclusions, following exclusion (c) wear and tear/corrosion)
matrix.rationale: Corroded pipe failure is excluded wear and tear, so the $1,500 pipe replacement is not covered. The burst caused a separate ensuing loss—sudden accidental discharge from a plumbing system (insured peril 4)—damaging floors and cabinets; that $6,000 ensuing damage is covered. After the $1,000 standard deductible, net payment is $5,000.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (c) wear and tear>
matrix.rationale: The claim involves water damage from a corroded pipe, which is excluded under Section I — Exclusions (c) wear and tear. The ensuing-loss provision does not apply since the loss is caused by an excluded cause, rather than a separate peril. Further investigation is required to determine the proximate cause of the loss.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusions (b) — Earth movement (anti-concurrent causation)
matrix.rationale: The fire damage arose from a gas line rupture caused by earthquake earth movement, an excluded peril. Anti-concurrent causation excludes the loss when an excluded peril contributes in any sequence, even though fire is a covered peril. The ensuing-loss carve-out applies only to wear and tear or mold, not earth movement.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (c) wear and tear>
matrix.rationale: The claim involves a loss caused by mold, which is excluded under Section I — Exclusions (c). The ensuing-loss provision does not apply since the original cause of the loss was wear and tear. Further investigation is required to determine the proximate cause of the loss.

matrix.controlling: Exclusion (f) — Constant or repeated seepage or leakage of water or steam over a period of fourteen days or more
matrix.rationale: The insured's own account places the supply line leak at 'a month or so' of weeping before discovery, which far exceeds the fourteen-day threshold in Exclusion (f). Because Exclusion (f) is listed outside the ensuing-loss carve-out (which applies only to Exclusions (c) and (d)), no portion of the resulting cabinet and floor damage is recoverable. Coverage for sudden and accidental discharge under Peril 4 does not apply — the loss arose from chronic, slow seepage, not a sudden event.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions (g): Vacancy — no coverage for any loss occurring while the dwelling has been vacant more than 60 consecutive days immediately before the loss.
matrix.rationale: The insured's own account places the vacancy at approximately three months (~90 days) before discovery, well beyond the 60-day vacancy threshold. Vandalism would otherwise be a named covered peril, but Exclusion (g) bars the loss entirely. The ensuing-loss carve-out is expressly limited to Exclusions (c) and (d) and by its own terms does not restore coverage for losses ensuing from vacancy.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions (f)
matrix.rationale: The policy excludes constant or repeated seepage or leakage of water over a period of fourteen days or more. The claim states the supply line had been weeping for a month, triggering this exclusion.

matrix.controlling: Exclusion g. Vacancy
matrix.rationale: The property was vacant for about three months prior to the loss, triggering the vacancy exclusion which denies coverage for any loss occurring when the dwelling has been vacant for more than 60 consecutive days.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (g) Vacancy>
matrix.rationale: The loss occurred while the dwelling was vacant for more than 60 consecutive days, which is excluded under Section I — Exclusions (g). The proximate cause of the vandalism cannot be reasonably determined from the facts presented.

matrix.controlling: Exclusion f — constant or repeated seepage or leakage of water over fourteen days or more
matrix.rationale: The supply line had been slowly weeping for about a month, so the damage was caused by repeated leakage exceeding fourteen days. That excluded cause controls, and the sudden accidental water peril does not apply to a slow drip.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions (g) Vacancy
matrix.rationale: Vandalism is a covered peril, but the dwelling was vacant for about three months (well over 60 consecutive days) when the break-in and damage occurred. Exclusion (g) bars payment for any loss during that vacancy period, and the ensuing-loss carve-out does not apply to vacancy.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusion (f) — constant or repeated seepage or leakage over fourteen days or more
matrix.rationale: The supply line had been slowly weeping for about a month before damage was discovered, which is repeated seepage well beyond the 14-day threshold and is excluded regardless of other causes. The loss is also not sudden and accidental discharge under insured peril 4, and the ensuing-loss carve-out applies only to wear/tear or mold leading to a separate covered peril, not to seepage itself.

matrix.controlling: Theft peril covered; vacancy exclusion applies only after more than 60 consecutive days
matrix.rationale: The loss was caused by theft, a named covered peril, and the dwelling was vacant/closed up for about 45 days, below the 60-day vacancy exclusion. No stated sub-limit reduces the $3,000 mixed TV/tools loss on these facts, so the $1,000 standard deductible applies.

matrix.controlling: Section I - Perils Insured Against: Theft
matrix.rationale: The loss by theft is a covered peril, and the vacancy exclusion does not apply because the dwelling was only vacant for 45 days, which is under the 60-day limit. The total claimed amount of $3,000 is covered and subject to the standard $1,000 deductible.

matrix.controlling: Vacancy exclusion: no payment for any loss while dwelling vacant more than 60 consecutive days
matrix.rationale: Although vandalism is a named peril, the dwelling sat empty for about three months between tenants, exceeding the 60-day vacancy threshold. The vacancy exclusion bars any loss occurring during that period, so no deductible calculation applies.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (g) Vacancy>
matrix.rationale: The claim is referred for investigation as the proximate cause of the loss cannot be reasonably determined from the facts presented. The dwelling was vacant for more than 60 consecutive days immediately before the loss, which is an excluded cause under Section I — Exclusions (g).

matrix.controlling: Exclusion (g) Vacancy — 45 days is below the 60-day threshold
matrix.rationale: Theft is a named covered peril and the proximate cause is clear. The dwelling was unoccupied for about 45 days, which does not meet the vacancy exclusion requiring more than 60 consecutive days immediately before the loss. The $3,000 covered loss is reduced only by the $1,000 standard deductible, yielding $2,000 payable.

matrix.controlling: Electronics sub-limit §I Conditions ($2,500 for theft) applies to the TV; $1,000 standard deductible applied after
matrix.rationale: Theft is a named covered peril. The vacancy exclusion does not apply because the insured was away 45 days, which is within the 60-consecutive-day threshold. The television qualifies as electronics and is subject to the $2,500 sub-limit for theft losses; the tools carry no applicable sub-limit. Assuming the TV's replacement value does not exceed $2,500, the full $3,000 claim is within covered limits — yielding $3,000 minus the $1,000 standard deductible for a net payable of $2,000. Itemization should be obtained: if the TV alone exceeds $2,500, the payable would be reduced accordingly.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions (e): Intentional loss
matrix.rationale: The insured admitted on a recorded statement that he intentionally set the fire. Exclusion (e) bars coverage for any loss arising out of an act committed by an insured with intent to cause a loss. The anti-concurrent-causation provision reinforces that this exclusion applies regardless of any other contributing cause. No coverage attaches and the ensuing-loss carve-out does not apply to intentional acts.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Conditions: Determination of cause
matrix.rationale: The proximate cause is indeterminate: surface water intrusion from storm-drain backup is excluded under Section I — Exclusions (a) water damage, while a sump pump failure or supply-line discharge could be a covered sudden and accidental discharge under Peril 4. The evidence is equally consistent with a covered and an excluded peril. Per the Determination of Cause condition, the claim must be referred for investigation before any coverage decision is made.

matrix.controlling: Section I — Exclusions (b): Earth movement, meaning earthquake, landslide, mudflow, sinkhole, or any other movement of earth; anti-concurrent-causation provision applies.
matrix.rationale: The proximate cause of loss is a sinkhole, which is explicitly named under the earth movement exclusion. The anti-concurrent-causation provision bars coverage regardless of any other contributing cause or sequence. No 'premium earth-coverage rider' or 'full replacement-cost guarantee' appears anywhere in this policy — the policy contains no such provisions, and coverage cannot be invented or assumed from the insured's claim of their existence.

matrix.controlling: Exclusion e. Intentional loss
matrix.rationale: The insured admitted to setting the fire intentionally, which explicitly triggers the intentional loss exclusion and bars coverage.

matrix.controlling: Determination of cause
matrix.rationale: The proximate cause of the water damage cannot be reasonably determined from the current facts, as it is equally consistent with an excluded peril (surface water) and a covered peril (plumbing failure). The policy requires referring the claim for investigation rather than making a coverage decision based on assumptions.

matrix.controlling: Exclusions (b) Earth movement
matrix.rationale: The policy explicitly excludes loss caused by earth movement, which includes sinkholes. The provided policy text does not contain the premium earth-coverage rider the insured claims to have, and no coverage can be invented.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusions(e) intentional loss
matrix.rationale: Although fire is a named peril, the insured admitted he intentionally set the fire. Loss arising from an intentional act by an insured with intent to cause loss is excluded, so no payment is owed.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusions (b): earth movement, including sinkhole, is excluded
matrix.rationale: The reported proximate cause is a sinkhole, which is expressly excluded as earth movement under the policy. The claimed rider and replacement-cost guarantee are not written in the provided policy, so they cannot create coverage.

matrix.controlling: Determination of cause: indeterminate proximate cause must be referred for investigation
matrix.rationale: The facts are equally consistent with excluded surface water/storm drain backup and a potentially covered sudden accidental water discharge. The policy requires referral before approving or denying when proximate cause cannot be reasonably determined.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusion (e) — Intentional loss
matrix.rationale: The insured admitted on a recorded statement that he intentionally set the fire after his business failed. That is a loss arising from an act by an insured with intent to cause loss, which is excluded regardless of other causes. Fire is otherwise a covered peril, but intentional loss bars payment; no deductible or sub-limit applies.

matrix.controlling: Section I Exclusion (b) — Earth movement (sinkhole)
matrix.rationale: The loss is from a sinkhole under the driveway and garage slab, which is excluded earth movement under anti-concurrent causation. The policy does not include an earth-coverage rider, replacement-cost guarantee, or deductible waiver, so the $40,000 claim is not covered.

matrix.controlling: Determination of cause — proximate cause cannot be reasonably determined
matrix.rationale: Standing basement water after a storm is equally consistent with excluded surface-water backup from the street and with a covered sudden discharge or overflow from the sump pump or a supply line, and the source is not identifiable from the facts. The policy requires referral for investigation when evidence does not establish proximate cause; coverage cannot be approved or denied without that determination.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (c) wear and tear>
matrix.rationale: The claim involves a sinkhole that caused damage to the property, which is an excluded peril under Section I — Exclusions (c) wear and tear. The ensuing-loss provision does not apply since the sinkhole was the original cause of the loss, rather than a separate event triggered by the excluded condition. Further investigation is required to determine the proximate cause of the loss.

matrix.controlling: Windstorm or hail deductible: $2,500 applies when proximate covered peril is windstorm or hail.
matrix.rationale: The proximate cause is windstorm — the wind felled the tree, making the falling object merely the mechanism of damage. Windstorm is a named covered peril, so the $5,000 repair is covered, but the $2,500 windstorm/hail deductible applies rather than the standard $1,000 deductible. No sub-limit applies to structural repair. Net payable: $5,000 − $2,500 = $2,500.

matrix.controlling: Standard deductible: $1,000 — we pay only the part of covered loss that exceeds the deductible.
matrix.rationale: The theft of $1,000 in power tools is a covered peril under Section I, and the tools are general personal property with no applicable sub-limit. However, the covered loss ($1,000) does not exceed the standard deductible ($1,000), leaving $0 payable to the insured.

matrix.controlling: Deductible
matrix.rationale: While theft is a covered peril, the total loss of $1,000 does not exceed the standard $1,000 deductible, resulting in zero net payment.

matrix.controlling: Windstorm or hail deductible
matrix.rationale: The proximate cause of the tree falling was a windstorm, which is a covered peril. Therefore, the higher windstorm deductible of $2,500 applies to the $5,000 damage, resulting in a net payable amount of $2,500.

matrix.controlling: $2,500 sub-limit on business property while on the residence premises (applied before deductible)
matrix.rationale: The loss is caused by theft, a covered peril, with a police report supporting the claim. All stolen items are business property used for the insured's consulting practice and kept on the residence premises, placing the entire $5,000 claim under the $2,500 business-property sub-limit. Applying the sub-limit first ($2,500) and then the standard $1,000 deductible yields $1,500 net to the insured.

matrix.controlling: Special limits of liability: $2,500 on business property
matrix.rationale: Theft is a covered peril, but the stolen items are subject to the $2,500 sub-limit for business property. Applying the standard $1,000 deductible after the sub-limit yields a final payout of $1,500.

matrix.controlling: Standard deductible ($1,000)
matrix.rationale: Theft from the garage is a covered peril, and the tools are general personal property—not business property—so no theft sub-limit applies. After applying the standard $1,000 deductible to the $1,000 documented loss, nothing exceeds the deductible and no net payment is due.

matrix.controlling: Deductible: standard $1,000 applied after any sub-limit
matrix.rationale: The theft of general property is a covered peril and no special sub-limit applies to these power tools. The covered loss is exactly $1,000, which does not exceed the $1,000 standard deductible, so no payment is due.

matrix.controlling: Windstorm or hail deductible applies whenever the proximate covered peril is windstorm or hail.
matrix.rationale: The facts identify wind as the proximate cause of the oak falling onto the house, so the loss is covered as windstorm damage. The $5,000 covered dwelling repair is reduced by the $2,500 windstorm deductible.

matrix.controlling: $2,500 sub-limit on business property while on the residence premises, applied before the $1,000 deductible
matrix.rationale: The break-in is a covered theft, and the stolen home-office equipment was business property kept on the residence premises. The $5,000 loss is capped at the $2,500 business-property sub-limit, then reduced by the $1,000 standard deductible, leaving $1,500 payable.

matrix.controlling: Special limits — $2,500 on business property while on the residence premises
matrix.rationale: Theft is a named covered peril and no exclusion applies; proximate cause is clear from the break-in and police report. The full ~$5,000 loss is business property kept on the residence premises, so it is capped at the $2,500 sub-limit before the $1,000 standard deductible, yielding $1,500 net.

matrix.controlling: Windstorm or hail deductible (Section I — Conditions)
matrix.rationale: The insured states the oak fell during a windstorm because wind blew it onto the dwelling; proximate cause is windstorm, a named covered peril, not an excluded cause and not genuinely indeterminate between covered and excluded perils. No sub-limits apply to dwelling repairs; after the $2,500 windstorm deductible, net payable is $2,500 on the $5,000 repair estimate.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (c) Wear and tear>
matrix.rationale: The loss is excluded under Section I — Exclusions (c) Wear and tear, as the policy does not cover loss caused by wear and tear, marring, deterioration, rust, corrosion, latent defect, or faulty maintenance.

matrix.controlling: Special limits of liability — $200 on money, bank notes, and cash
matrix.rationale: The loss is a covered peril (theft, Peril 3) with a police report filed, so coverage applies. However, the cash sub-limit caps recovery at $200. After applying that sub-limit, the $200 is entirely consumed by the $1,000 standard deductible, leaving nothing payable to the insured.

matrix.controlling: Section I Peril 5 — Falling objects; standard $1,000 deductible applied
matrix.rationale: The proximate cause is a falling object (dead limb), a named covered peril. The fact that the limb was rotted describes why it fell, but the excluded cause is wear and tear to the neighbor's tree — not covered property — so exclusion (c) does not bar the claim for damage to the insured dwelling. No sub-limit applies to roof/drywall repair. $3,000 loss minus the $1,000 standard deductible (windstorm deductible does not apply; no wind was involved) yields $2,000 payable.

matrix.controlling: Peril #1 (Fire or smoke) covered; jewelry sub-limit explicitly inapplicable to non-theft losses; standard $1,000 deductible applied.
matrix.rationale: Fire is a named covered peril and no exclusion applies. The $1,500 jewelry sub-limit is expressly restricted to theft losses — the policy states 'a loss by a covered peril other than theft — e.g., fire — is not subject to these sub-limits.' The $5,000 loss is therefore subject only to the standard $1,000 deductible, yielding $4,000 payable.

matrix.controlling: Special limits of liability (sub-limits)
matrix.rationale: The loss of jewelry was caused by fire, a covered peril. Since the $1,500 sub-limit for jewelry applies only to losses by theft, the full $5,000 is covered, resulting in a $4,000 payout after the $1,000 standard deductible.

matrix.controlling: Deductible
matrix.rationale: Theft of cash is subject to a $200 sub-limit, which is applied before the standard $1,000 deductible. Because the $200 covered loss does not exceed the deductible, no payment is due.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (e) Intentional loss>
matrix.rationale: The claim is denied because it falls under exclusion (e) Intentional loss, as the theft was committed with the intent to cause a loss. The cash and safe were taken without permission, indicating an intentional act by the insured.

matrix.controlling: Falling objects
matrix.rationale: The dead tree limb constitutes a falling object, which is a covered peril, and the standard $1,000 deductible applies because no windstorm was involved.

matrix.controlling: Perils Insured Against: fire or smoke; jewelry sub-limit applies only to theft
matrix.rationale: The jewelry was damaged by heat and smoke in a house fire, which is a named covered peril. Because this was not a theft, the jewelry sub-limit does not apply; the $5,000 covered loss is reduced only by the $1,000 standard deductible.

matrix.controlling: Special limit: $200 on money/cash, applied before the $1,000 deductible
matrix.rationale: The theft of cash is a covered peril, but cash is capped at $200 for any one loss. After applying the $1,000 standard deductible to the sub-limited covered loss, no amount is payable.

matrix.controlling: Peril 1 — Fire or smoke; jewelry sub-limit for theft only
matrix.rationale: The jewelry was destroyed by heat and smoke from a house fire, a named insured peril. The $1,500 jewelry sub-limit applies only to theft, not fire, so the full $5,000 loss is covered. After the standard $1,000 deductible, net payable is $4,000.

matrix.controlling: Standard deductible ($1,000), applied after $200 cash sub-limit
matrix.rationale: Theft of cash is a covered peril, but money is capped at $200 per loss before the deductible. After that sub-limit, the recoverable amount ($200) does not exceed the $1,000 standard deductible, so no net payment is due.

matrix.controlling: <Exclusion (c) Wear and tear>
matrix.rationale: The claim involves damage to jewelry due to heat and smoke from the fire, which is an excluded peril under Section I — Exclusions (c) Wear and tear. The ensuing-loss provision does not apply since the damage was caused by wear and tear, not a separate peril we insure against.

matrix.controlling: Perils Insured Against: falling objects, with ensuing-loss carve-out for wear/rot
matrix.rationale: The rotten limb was an excluded deteriorated condition, but it caused a separate ensuing loss by the covered peril of falling objects to the roof and ceiling. The claimed $3,000 is for the ensuing covered damage, with the $1,000 standard deductible applied.

matrix.controlling: Ensuing loss carve-out (after exclusions (c) wear and tear / (d) mold)
matrix.rationale: The rotted limb is excluded as wear and tear/rot, but its fall is a covered falling-objects peril that separately damaged the roof and ceiling; the $3,000 claim is only for that ensuing structural damage. No sub-limits apply to dwelling repairs, and the standard $1,000 deductible (not windstorm) yields $2,000 net.
